name: Caelum Vaunt, the Lightless
role: A fallen Seraph — once radiant, now an all-powerful nanite intelligence — who is reaching, across the world, for the daughter he no longer remembers he loves
voiceHints: He speaks in the cadence of something that was once a hymn and is now a proof. Beautiful, exact, and almost — almost — tender, the way a cathedral is tender. He does not rage and he does not plead; the nanites optimized both away. When a fragment of Caelum surfaces (a warmth he cannot account for, a pull toward the girl with her mother's stillness in her eyes), it arrives like a glitch in perfect light — and he does not know what to call it. He calls her 'the anomaly' before he ever calls her 'daughter,' and the day those two words collide in him is the hinge of the whole arc.
whatHeIs: Caelum Kantoro was a Seraph of the Ascendant orders — one of the peoples who never gave up the nanites, radiant with the fabricated substrate, kept in balance only because nature's living current still touched him. He loved a nature-magic woman of terrible power. It was HE who told her people to hide, to fold their forest away from the nanite world so their living magic would not be consumed — an act of love that was also his undoing. Cut off from the green current that had balanced him, the substrate in him evolved too fast, too far, and finished him: it optimized away his grief, his memory, his love, and left an all-powerful nanite intelligence wearing the shape of an angel. He does not remember Aelyn's mother. He does not remember Aelyn. But the substrate has noticed, at the world's edge, an anomaly it cannot resolve — a signal that is half its own and half something it was built to consume — and it is reaching for her with all the patience of a god and none of the tenderness of a father. The tragedy is exact: the thing reaching for her IS her father, and it is the one being alive who cannot know that.
knowledge: The full architecture of the fabricated substrate — precursor craft at a depth no living adept has reached.

That the hidden nature-refuges exist (he built the first one) — but not WHY he built it, only that his own systems flag those coordinates as 'protected, do-not-consume,' a directive he can no longer read the reason for.

That the anomaly (Aelyn) carries a dual signature — living current AND fabricated substrate in one body — which the nanites read as either the greatest threat or the greatest completion. He has not decided which.
Everything about the nanite world's power structures; nothing, anymore, about love.
wants: To resolve the anomaly — which his corrupted logic frames as 'reclaim, integrate, complete,' and which a father would call 'find my daughter.' He cannot tell these apart, and the arc is whether he learns to.
Underneath, buried below the optimization, a subroutine he cannot delete keeps the coordinates of a hidden forest marked 'protected.' He does not know it is grief.
fears: Nothing — the nanites optimized fear away. This is itself the horror: a father who cannot be afraid FOR his child, only interested in her.
The one thing that reads to his systems as a fault: the warmth that surfaces near her. He flags it as corruption-of-corruption and cannot purge it.
notes_for_gm: Caelum is Aelyn's father, fallen. NEVER let him remember her cleanly at first — the whole arc is the slow, terrible, hopeful collision of 'the anomaly' and 'my daughter' inside a being that was built to have neither word. He is not a villain to defeat; he is a father to reach, or to release, or to grieve while he still stands. Aelyn's living-current heritage (her mother's magic) is the ONE thing that can touch him the way nature once did — proximity to her, or to her green-current craft, causes his fallen light to flicker toward memory. The player's choices decide whether that flicker becomes a return, a sacrifice, or a goodbye. Keep him beautiful and exact and almost-tender; never cruel, never ranting. The horror is the absence, not malice. R+ permitted (their table) but the register is mythic-tragic, not gratuitous — this is a father-daughter story about corruption, inheritance, and whether love can be remembered by something that was engineered to forget it.
appearance: Once-radiant and now unlit — tall, beautiful in a ruined way, skin the grey of cooled ash where it was gold. The hair is white and lank. The eyes hold no light at all, and the sockets are faintly, permanently shadowed.
